{{ill|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|ja|中山茶臼山古墳}} {{Infobox ancient site |name = Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun |image = [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound (rear circular section on the right, front rectangular section on the left) |affiliation = |location = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}} {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}} {{ill|Kita-ku (Okayama)|ja|北区}} Kibitsu |latitude_degrees = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 0.51 |longitude_degrees = 133|longitude_minutes = 51|longitude_seconds = 16.87 |ISO = JP-33 |type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size = Mound length: 105 m
Height: 11 m (rear circular section) |construction_period = Early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} |burial_facility = Unknown |buried_person = (Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命}} |artifacts = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |mausoleum = Designated as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命}} by the Imperial Household Agency |noteworthy_features = Identified as half the proportional size of {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}} ({{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}) |map = Japan Okayama |label = Nakayama Chausuyama
Kofun |icon = Zenpokoenfun-South |label_position = top }} [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Worship site for Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto's tomb}}]] '''Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun''' (中山茶臼山古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kita-ku (Okayama)|ja|北区}}, {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus). The actual person buried here is unknown, but the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the mound as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命}}, a son of Emperor Kōrei, the 7th emperor of Japan. == Overview == Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus built on the summit of the independent hill range known as "Kibi no Nakayama" to the west of Okayama City. The mound is exceptionally well-preserved to this day.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} It is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}, which conducted a mound measurement survey in 2008.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus features a "paddle-shaped" keyhole design, with the front rectangular section facing south.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound is constructed in two tiers.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} Surface features include paving stones and evidence suggesting the presence of a haniwa (clay figurine) row based on haniwa fragments.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} However, further details remain unclear. The mound is estimated to have been built during the early Kofun period, though precise dating remains uncertain due to limited evidence.[http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] (Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Cultural Properties"). Its location combines coastal views of Kibitsu and inland plains, suggesting both maritime and inland significance.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus is situated on the historical boundary between Bizen and Bitchū Provinces. Other nearby tumuli in Kibi no Nakayama include early-period tumuli such as {{ill|Yatōji Yama Kofun|ja|矢藤治山古墳}} and mid-period tumuli such as {{ill|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|ja|尾上車山古墳}} (a designated national historic site). == Historical Record == * 1874 (Meiji 7): Designated as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Excavation monitoring during torii reconstruction; Yayoi pottery discovered.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 2008: Mound measurement survey conducted by the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} == Mound Dimensions == [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Rear mound of the tumulus}}{{small|Foreground: An Observation Kannon shrine at Hattoku-ji Okuin, speculated to be a former {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}}.}}]] The mound's dimensions, based on Imperial Household Agency measurements, are as follows:{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * Mound length: 105 m * Rear circular section: Elliptical (axes misaligned with the main axis of the mound). Two tiers. ** Diameter: Long axis 68 m, short axis 64 m ** Height: 11 m * Front rectangular section: Two tiers. ** Length: 63 m ** Width: Approximately 45 m ** Height: 5 m * Constriction section: ** Width: Approximately 23 m Earlier measurements estimated a mound length of approximately 120 m, but the 2008 survey revised this to the above dimensions.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound's design is considered to mirror half the proportional size of the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}} == Buried Person == The actual buried individual remains unknown. However, the Imperial Household Agency designates the mound as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto, a son of Emperor Kōrei.Imperial Ministry's Bureau of Mausolea [{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 'Ryo Bo Yōran'] (1934, {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}} Digital Collection).'Mausoleum Topography Collection, Compact Edition,' Imperial Household Agency's Bureau of Mausolea, Gakuseisha, 2014, p. 400. Historical texts, including the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, reference Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto as a conqueror of Kibi Province and a participant in the {{ill|Shido Shogun|ja|四道将軍}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}}}}]] == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|（Related literature not directly used in this article）}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1988|chapter=Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} Vol. 34 Okayama Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4-582-49034-4|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Tokusho Idemiya|year=1989|chapter=Chausuyama Kofun > Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tōkyōdō Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4-490-10260-7|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Masaki Ueda|year=|chapter=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Hikoisaseri|title=Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan names|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=978-4-642-01458-8|ref={{Harvid|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinichi Yakushiji|year=2008|chapter=Walking and Thinking Along Kibi Roads Vol. 1|title=吉備の古墳 上|publisher=Kibito Publishing|isbn=|ref=}} == Related Links == {{Commonscat|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun}} * [http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] - Kofun |icon = Zenpokoenfun-South |label_position = top }} [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Worship site for Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto's tomb}}]] '''Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun''' (中山茶臼山古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kita-ku (Okayama)|ja|北区}}, {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus). The actual person buried here is unknown, but the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the mound as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命}}, a son of Emperor Kōrei, the 7th emperor of Japan. == Overview == Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus built on the summit of the independent hill range known as "Kibi no Nakayama" to the west of Okayama City. The mound is exceptionally well-preserved to this day.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} It is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}, which conducted a mound measurement survey in 2008.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus features a "paddle-shaped" keyhole design, with the front rectangular section facing south.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound is constructed in two tiers.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} Surface features include paving stones and evidence suggesting the presence of a haniwa (clay figurine) row based on haniwa fragments.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} However, further details remain unclear. The mound is estimated to have been built during the early Kofun period, though precise dating remains uncertain due to limited evidence.[http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] (Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Cultural Properties"). Its location combines coastal views of Kibitsu and inland plains, suggesting both maritime and inland significance.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus is situated on the historical boundary between Bizen and Bitchū Provinces. Other nearby tumuli in Kibi no Nakayama include early-period tumuli such as {{ill|Yatōji Yama Kofun|ja|矢藤治山古墳}} and mid-period tumuli such as {{ill|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|ja|尾上車山古墳}} (a designated national historic site). == Historical Record == * 1874 (Meiji 7): Designated as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Excavation monitoring during torii reconstruction; Yayoi pottery discovered.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 2008: Mound measurement survey conducted by the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} == Mound Dimensions == [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Rear mound of the tumulus}}{{small|Foreground: An Observation Kannon shrine at Hattoku-ji Okuin, speculated to be a former {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}}.}}]] The mound's dimensions, based on Imperial Household Agency measurements, are as follows:{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * Mound length: 105 m * Rear circular section: Elliptical (axes misaligned with the main axis of the mound). Two tiers. ** Diameter: Long axis 68 m, short axis 64 m ** Height: 11 m * Front rectangular section: Two tiers. ** Length: 63 m ** Width: Approximately 45 m ** Height: 5 m * Constriction section: ** Width: Approximately 23 m Earlier measurements estimated a mound length of approximately 120 m, but the 2008 survey revised this to the above dimensions.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound's design is considered to mirror half the proportional size of the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}} == Buried Person == The actual buried individual remains unknown. However, the Imperial Household Agency designates the mound as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto, a son of Emperor Kōrei.Imperial Ministry's Bureau of Mausolea [{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 'Ryo Bo Yōran'] (1934, {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}} Digital Collection).'Mausoleum Topography Collection, Compact Edition,' Imperial Household Agency's Bureau of Mausolea, Gakuseisha, 2014, p. 400. Historical texts, including the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, reference Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto as a conqueror of Kibi Province and a participant in the {{ill|Shido Shogun|ja|四道将軍}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}}}}]] == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|（Related literature not directly used in this article）}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1988|chapter=Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} Vol. 34 Okayama Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4-582-49034-4|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Tokusho Idemiya|year=1989|chapter=Chausuyama Kofun > Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tōkyōdō Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4-490-10260-7|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Masaki Ueda|year=|chapter=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Hikoisaseri|title=Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan names|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=978-4-642-01458-8|ref={{Harvid|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinichi Yakushiji|year=2008|chapter=Walking and Thinking Along Kibi Roads Vol. 1|title=吉備の古墳 上|publisher=Kibito Publishing|isbn=|ref=}} == Related Links == {{Commonscat|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun}} * [http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] - Its location combines coastal views of Kibitsu and inland plains, suggesting both maritime and inland significance.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus is situated on the historical boundary between Bizen and Bitchū Provinces. Other nearby tumuli in Kibi no Nakayama include early-period tumuli such as {{ill|Yatōji Yama Kofun|ja|矢藤治山古墳}} and mid-period tumuli such as {{ill|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|ja|尾上車山古墳}} (a designated national historic site). == Historical Record == * 1874 (Meiji 7): Designated as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Excavation monitoring during torii reconstruction; Yayoi pottery discovered.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 2008: Mound measurement survey conducted by the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} == Mound Dimensions == [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Rear mound of the tumulus}}{{small|Foreground: An Observation Kannon shrine at Hattoku-ji Okuin, speculated to be a former {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}}.}}]] The mound's dimensions, based on Imperial Household Agency measurements, are as follows:{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * Mound length: 105 m * Rear circular section: Elliptical (axes misaligned with the main axis of the mound). Two tiers. ** Diameter: Long axis 68 m, short axis 64 m ** Height: 11 m * Front rectangular section: Two tiers. ** Length: 63 m ** Width: Approximately 45 m ** Height: 5 m * Constriction section: ** Width: Approximately 23 m Earlier measurements estimated a mound length of approximately 120 m, but the 2008 survey revised this to the above dimensions.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound's design is considered to mirror half the proportional size of the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}} == Buried Person == The actual buried individual remains unknown. However, the Imperial Household Agency designates the mound as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto, a son of Emperor Kōrei.Imperial Ministry's Bureau of Mausolea [{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 'Ryo Bo Yōran'] (1934, {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}} Digital Collection).'Mausoleum Topography Collection, Compact Edition,' Imperial Household Agency's Bureau of Mausolea, Gakuseisha, 2014, p. 400. Historical texts, including the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, reference Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto as a conqueror of Kibi Province and a participant in the {{ill|Shido Shogun|ja|四道将軍}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}}}}]] == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|（Related literature not directly used in this article）}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1988|chapter=Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} Vol. 34 Okayama Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4-582-49034-4|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Tokusho Idemiya|year=1989|chapter=Chausuyama Kofun > Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tōkyōdō Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4-490-10260-7|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Masaki Ueda|year=|chapter=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Hikoisaseri|title=Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan names|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=978-4-642-01458-8|ref={{Harvid|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinichi Yakushiji|year=2008|chapter=Walking and Thinking Along Kibi Roads Vol. 1|title=吉備の古墳 上|publisher=Kibito Publishing|isbn=|ref=}} == Related Links == {{Commonscat|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun}} * [http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] - Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Cultural Properties" [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kita-ku, Okayama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Imperial Mausolea of Japan]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[ja:中山茶臼山古墳]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] {{Kofun navbar}} [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:%E4%B8%AD%E5%B1%B1%E8%8C%B6%E8%87%BC%E5%B1%B1%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3]] [[Category:null category]][[Category:new pages most likely untranslated or similar]] [[Category:前方後円墳]][[sn:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:前方後円墳]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]]

{{ill|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|ja|中山茶臼山古墳}} {{Infobox ancient site |name = Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun |image = [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound (rear circular section on the right, front rectangular section on the left) |affiliation = |location = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}} {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}} {{ill|Kita-ku (Okayama)|ja|北区}} Kibitsu |latitude_degrees = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 0.51 |longitude_degrees = 133|longitude_minutes = 51|longitude_seconds = 16.87 |ISO = JP-33 |type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size = Mound length: 105 m
Height: 11 m (rear circular section) |construction_period = Early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} |burial_facility = Unknown |buried_person = (Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命}} |artifacts = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |mausoleum = Designated as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命}} by the Imperial Household Agency |noteworthy_features = Identified as half the proportional size of {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}} ({{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}) |map = Japan Okayama |label = Nakayama Chausuyama
Kofun |icon = Zenpokoenfun-South |label_position = top }} [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Worship site for Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto's tomb}}]] '''Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun''' (中山茶臼山古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kita-ku (Okayama)|ja|北区}}, {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus). The actual person buried here is unknown, but the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the mound as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命}}, a son of Emperor Kōrei, the 7th emperor of Japan. == Overview == Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus built on the summit of the independent hill range known as "Kibi no Nakayama" to the west of Okayama City. The mound is exceptionally well-preserved to this day.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} It is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}, which conducted a mound measurement survey in 2008.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus features a "paddle-shaped" keyhole design, with the front rectangular section facing south.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound is constructed in two tiers.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} Surface features include paving stones and evidence suggesting the presence of a haniwa (clay figurine) row based on haniwa fragments.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} However, further details remain unclear. The mound is estimated to have been built during the early Kofun period, though precise dating remains uncertain due to limited evidence.[http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] (Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Cultural Properties"). Its location combines coastal views of Kibitsu and inland plains, suggesting both maritime and inland significance.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus is situated on the historical boundary between Bizen and Bitchū Provinces. Other nearby tumuli in Kibi no Nakayama include early-period tumuli such as {{ill|Yatōji Yama Kofun|ja|矢藤治山古墳}} and mid-period tumuli such as {{ill|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|ja|尾上車山古墳}} (a designated national historic site). == Historical Record == * 1874 (Meiji 7): Designated as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Excavation monitoring during torii reconstruction; Yayoi pottery discovered.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 2008: Mound measurement survey conducted by the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} == Mound Dimensions == [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Rear mound of the tumulus}}{{small|Foreground: An Observation Kannon shrine at Hattoku-ji Okuin, speculated to be a former {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}}.}}]] The mound's dimensions, based on Imperial Household Agency measurements, are as follows:{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * Mound length: 105 m * Rear circular section: Elliptical (axes misaligned with the main axis of the mound). Two tiers. ** Diameter: Long axis 68 m, short axis 64 m ** Height: 11 m * Front rectangular section: Two tiers. ** Length: 63 m ** Width: Approximately 45 m ** Height: 5 m * Constriction section: ** Width: Approximately 23 m Earlier measurements estimated a mound length of approximately 120 m, but the 2008 survey revised this to the above dimensions.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound's design is considered to mirror half the proportional size of the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}} == Buried Person == The actual buried individual remains unknown. However, the Imperial Household Agency designates the mound as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto, a son of Emperor Kōrei.Imperial Ministry's Bureau of Mausolea [{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 'Ryo Bo Yōran'] (1934, {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}} Digital Collection).'Mausoleum Topography Collection, Compact Edition,' Imperial Household Agency's Bureau of Mausolea, Gakuseisha, 2014, p. 400. Historical texts, including the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, reference Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto as a conqueror of Kibi Province and a participant in the {{ill|Shido Shogun|ja|四道将軍}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}}}}]] == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|（Related literature not directly used in this article）}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1988|chapter=Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} Vol. 34 Okayama Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4-582-49034-4|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Tokusho Idemiya|year=1989|chapter=Chausuyama Kofun > Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tōkyōdō Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4-490-10260-7|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Masaki Ueda|year=|chapter=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Hikoisaseri|title=Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan names|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=978-4-642-01458-8|ref={{Harvid|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinichi Yakushiji|year=2008|chapter=Walking and Thinking Along Kibi Roads Vol. 1|title=吉備の古墳 上|publisher=Kibito Publishing|isbn=|ref=}} == Related Links == {{Commonscat|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun}} * [http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] - Two tiers. ** Diameter: Long axis 68 m, short axis 64 m ** Height: 11 m * Front rectangular section: Two tiers. ** Length: 63 m ** Width: Approximately 45 m ** Height: 5 m * Constriction section: ** Width: Approximately 23 m Earlier measurements estimated a mound length of approximately 120 m, but the 2008 survey revised this to the above dimensions.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound's design is considered to mirror half the proportional size of the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}} == Buried Person == The actual buried individual remains unknown. However, the Imperial Household Agency designates the mound as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto, a son of Emperor Kōrei.Imperial Ministry's Bureau of Mausolea [{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 'Ryo Bo Yōran'] (1934, {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}} Digital Collection).'Mausoleum Topography Collection, Compact Edition,' Imperial Household Agency's Bureau of Mausolea, Gakuseisha, 2014, p. 400. Historical texts, including the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, reference Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto as a conqueror of Kibi Province and a participant in the {{ill|Shido Shogun|ja|四道将軍}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}}}}]] == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|（Related literature not directly used in this article）}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1988|chapter=Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} Vol. 34 Okayama Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4-582-49034-4|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Tokusho Idemiya|year=1989|chapter=Chausuyama Kofun > Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tōkyōdō Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4-490-10260-7|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Masaki Ueda|year=|chapter=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Hikoisaseri|title=Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan names|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=978-4-642-01458-8|ref={{Harvid|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinichi Yakushiji|year=2008|chapter=Walking and Thinking Along Kibi Roads Vol. 1|title=吉備の古墳 上|publisher=Kibito Publishing|isbn=|ref=}} == Related Links == {{Commonscat|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun}} * [http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] - {{ill|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|ja|中山茶臼山古墳}} {{Infobox ancient site |name = Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun |image = [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound (rear circular section on the right, front rectangular section on the left) |affiliation = |location = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}} {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}} {{ill|Kita-ku (Okayama)|ja|北区}} Kibitsu |latitude_degrees = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 0.51 |longitude_degrees = 133|longitude_minutes = 51|longitude_seconds = 16.87 |ISO = JP-33 |type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size = Mound length: 105 m
Height: 11 m (rear circular section) |construction_period = Early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} |burial_facility = Unknown |buried_person = (Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命}} |artifacts = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |mausoleum = Designated as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命}} by the Imperial Household Agency |noteworthy_features = Identified as half the proportional size of {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}} ({{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}) |map = Japan Okayama |label = Nakayama Chausuyama
Kofun |icon = Zenpokoenfun-South |label_position = top }} [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Worship site for Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto's tomb}}]] '''Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun''' (中山茶臼山古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kita-ku (Okayama)|ja|北区}}, {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus). The actual person buried here is unknown, but the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the mound as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命}}, a son of Emperor Kōrei, the 7th emperor of Japan. == Overview == Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus built on the summit of the independent hill range known as "Kibi no Nakayama" to the west of Okayama City. The mound is exceptionally well-preserved to this day.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} It is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}, which conducted a mound measurement survey in 2008.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus features a "paddle-shaped" keyhole design, with the front rectangular section facing south.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound is constructed in two tiers.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} Surface features include paving stones and evidence suggesting the presence of a haniwa (clay figurine) row based on haniwa fragments.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} However, further details remain unclear. The mound is estimated to have been built during the early Kofun period, though precise dating remains uncertain due to limited evidence.[http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] (Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Cultural Properties"). Its location combines coastal views of Kibitsu and inland plains, suggesting both maritime and inland significance.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus is situated on the historical boundary between Bizen and Bitchū Provinces. Other nearby tumuli in Kibi no Nakayama include early-period tumuli such as {{ill|Yatōji Yama Kofun|ja|矢藤治山古墳}} and mid-period tumuli such as {{ill|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|ja|尾上車山古墳}} (a designated national historic site). == Historical Record == * 1874 (Meiji 7): Designated as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Excavation monitoring during torii reconstruction; Yayoi pottery discovered.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 2008: Mound measurement survey conducted by the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} == Mound Dimensions == [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Rear mound of the tumulus}}{{small|Foreground: An Observation Kannon shrine at Hattoku-ji Okuin, speculated to be a former {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}}.}}]] The mound's dimensions, based on Imperial Household Agency measurements, are as follows:{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * Mound length: 105 m * Rear circular section: Elliptical (axes misaligned with the main axis of the mound). Two tiers. ** Diameter: Long axis 68 m, short axis 64 m ** Height: 11 m * Front rectangular section: Two tiers. ** Length: 63 m ** Width: Approximately 45 m ** Height: 5 m * Constriction section: ** Width: Approximately 23 m Earlier measurements estimated a mound length of approximately 120 m, but the 2008 survey revised this to the above dimensions.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound's design is considered to mirror half the proportional size of the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}} == Buried Person == The actual buried individual remains unknown. However, the Imperial Household Agency designates the mound as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto, a son of Emperor Kōrei.Imperial Ministry's Bureau of Mausolea [{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 'Ryo Bo Yōran'] (1934, {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}} Digital Collection).'Mausoleum Topography Collection, Compact Edition,' Imperial Household Agency's Bureau of Mausolea, Gakuseisha, 2014, p. 400. Historical texts, including the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, reference Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto as a conqueror of Kibi Province and a participant in the {{ill|Shido Shogun|ja|四道将軍}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}}}}]] == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|（Related literature not directly used in this article）}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1988|chapter=Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} Vol. 34 Okayama Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4-582-49034-4|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Tokusho Idemiya|year=1989|chapter=Chausuyama Kofun > Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tōkyōdō Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4-490-10260-7|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Masaki Ueda|year=|chapter=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Hikoisaseri|title=Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan names|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=978-4-642-01458-8|ref={{Harvid|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinichi Yakushiji|year=2008|chapter=Walking and Thinking Along Kibi Roads Vol. 1|title=吉備の古墳 上|publisher=Kibito Publishing|isbn=|ref=}} == Related Links == {{Commonscat|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun}} * [http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] - Historical texts, including the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, reference Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto as a conqueror of Kibi Province and a participant in the {{ill|Shido Shogun|ja|四道将軍}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}}}}]] == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|（Related literature not directly used in this article）}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1988|chapter=Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} Vol. 34 Okayama Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4-582-49034-4|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Tokusho Idemiya|year=1989|chapter=Chausuyama Kofun > Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tōkyōdō Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4-490-10260-7|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Masaki Ueda|year=|chapter=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Hikoisaseri|title=Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan names|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=978-4-642-01458-8|ref={{Harvid|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinichi Yakushiji|year=2008|chapter=Walking and Thinking Along Kibi Roads Vol. 1|title=吉備の古墳 上|publisher=Kibito Publishing|isbn=|ref=}} == Related Links == {{Commonscat|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun}} * [http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] - Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Cultural Properties" [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kita-ku, Okayama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Imperial Mausolea of Japan]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[ja:中山茶臼山古墳]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] {{Kofun navbar}} [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:%E4%B8%AD%E5%B1%B1%E8%8C%B6%E8%87%BC%E5%B1%B1%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3]] [[Category:null category]][[Category:new pages most likely untranslated or similar]] [[sn:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun}}

{{ill|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|ja|中山茶臼山古墳}} {{Infobox ancient site |name = Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun |image = [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound (rear circular section on the right, front rectangular section on the left) |affiliation = |location = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}} {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}} {{ill|Kita-ku (Okayama)|ja|北区}} Kibitsu |latitude_degrees = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 0.51 |longitude_degrees = 133|longitude_minutes = 51|longitude_seconds = 16.87 |ISO = JP-33 |type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size = Mound length: 105 m
Height: 11 m (rear circular section) |construction_period = Early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} |burial_facility = Unknown |buried_person = (Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命}} |artifacts = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |mausoleum = Designated as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命}} by the Imperial Household Agency |noteworthy_features = Identified as half the proportional size of {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}} ({{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}) |map = Japan Okayama |label = Nakayama Chausuyama
Kofun |icon = Zenpokoenfun-South |label_position = top }} [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Worship site for Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto's tomb}}]] '''Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun''' (中山茶臼山古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kita-ku (Okayama)|ja|北区}}, {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus). The actual person buried here is unknown, but the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the mound as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命}}, a son of Emperor Kōrei, the 7th emperor of Japan. == Overview == Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus built on the summit of the independent hill range known as "Kibi no Nakayama" to the west of Okayama City. The mound is exceptionally well-preserved to this day.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} It is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}, which conducted a mound measurement survey in 2008.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus features a "paddle-shaped" keyhole design, with the front rectangular section facing south.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound is constructed in two tiers.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} Surface features include paving stones and evidence suggesting the presence of a haniwa (clay figurine) row based on haniwa fragments.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} However, further details remain unclear. The mound is estimated to have been built during the early Kofun period, though precise dating remains uncertain due to limited evidence.[http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] (Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Cultural Properties"). Its location combines coastal views of Kibitsu and inland plains, suggesting both maritime and inland significance.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus is situated on the historical boundary between Bizen and Bitchū Provinces. Other nearby tumuli in Kibi no Nakayama include early-period tumuli such as {{ill|Yatōji Yama Kofun|ja|矢藤治山古墳}} and mid-period tumuli such as {{ill|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|ja|尾上車山古墳}} (a designated national historic site). == Historical Record == * 1874 (Meiji 7): Designated as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Excavation monitoring during torii reconstruction; Yayoi pottery discovered.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 2008: Mound measurement survey conducted by the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} == Mound Dimensions == [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Rear mound of the tumulus}}{{small|Foreground: An Observation Kannon shrine at Hattoku-ji Okuin, speculated to be a former {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}}.}}]] The mound's dimensions, based on Imperial Household Agency measurements, are as follows:{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * Mound length: 105 m * Rear circular section: Elliptical (axes misaligned with the main axis of the mound). Two tiers. ** Diameter: Long axis 68 m, short axis 64 m ** Height: 11 m * Front rectangular section: Two tiers. ** Length: 63 m ** Width: Approximately 45 m ** Height: 5 m * Constriction section: ** Width: Approximately 23 m Earlier measurements estimated a mound length of approximately 120 m, but the 2008 survey revised this to the above dimensions.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound's design is considered to mirror half the proportional size of the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}} == Buried Person == The actual buried individual remains unknown. However, the Imperial Household Agency designates the mound as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto, a son of Emperor Kōrei.Imperial Ministry's Bureau of Mausolea [{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 'Ryo Bo Yōran'] (1934, {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}} Digital Collection).'Mausoleum Topography Collection, Compact Edition,' Imperial Household Agency's Bureau of Mausolea, Gakuseisha, 2014, p. 400. Historical texts, including the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, reference Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto as a conqueror of Kibi Province and a participant in the {{ill|Shido Shogun|ja|四道将軍}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}}}}]] == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|（Related literature not directly used in this article）}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1988|chapter=Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} Vol. 34 Okayama Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4-582-49034-4|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Tokusho Idemiya|year=1989|chapter=Chausuyama Kofun > Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tōkyōdō Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4-490-10260-7|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Masaki Ueda|year=|chapter=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Hikoisaseri|title=Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan names|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=978-4-642-01458-8|ref={{Harvid|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinichi Yakushiji|year=2008|chapter=Walking and Thinking Along Kibi Roads Vol. 1|title=吉備の古墳 上|publisher=Kibito Publishing|isbn=|ref=}} == Related Links == {{Commonscat|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun}} * [http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] - It is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus). The actual person buried here is unknown, but the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the mound as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命}}, a son of Emperor Kōrei, the 7th emperor of Japan. == Overview == Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus built on the summit of the independent hill range known as "Kibi no Nakayama" to the west of Okayama City. The mound is exceptionally well-preserved to this day.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} It is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}, which conducted a mound measurement survey in 2008.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus features a "paddle-shaped" keyhole design, with the front rectangular section facing south.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound is constructed in two tiers.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} Surface features include paving stones and evidence suggesting the presence of a haniwa (clay figurine) row based on haniwa fragments.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} However, further details remain unclear. Its location combines coastal views of Kibitsu and inland plains, suggesting both maritime and inland significance.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus is situated on the historical boundary between Bizen and Bitchū Provinces. Other nearby tumuli in Kibi no Nakayama include early-period tumuli such as {{ill|Yatōji Yama Kofun|ja|矢藤治山古墳}} and mid-period tumuli such as {{ill|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|ja|尾上車山古墳}} (a designated national historic site). == Historical Record == * 1874 (Meiji 7): Designated as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Excavation monitoring during torii reconstruction; Yayoi pottery discovered.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 2008: Mound measurement survey conducted by the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} == Mound Dimensions == [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Rear mound of the tumulus}}{{small|Foreground: An Observation Kannon shrine at Hattoku-ji Okuin, speculated to be a former {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}}.}}]] The mound's dimensions, based on Imperial Household Agency measurements, are as follows:{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * Mound length: 105 m * Rear circular section: Elliptical (axes misaligned with the main axis of the mound). Two tiers. ** Diameter: Long axis 68 m, short axis 64 m ** Height: 11 m * Front rectangular section: Two tiers. ** Length: 63 m ** Width: Approximately 45 m ** Height: 5 m * Constriction section: ** Width: Approximately 23 m Earlier measurements estimated a mound length of approximately 120 m, but the 2008 survey revised this to the above dimensions.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound's design is considered to mirror half the proportional size of the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}} == Buried Person == The actual buried individual remains unknown. However, the Imperial Household Agency designates the mound as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto, a son of Emperor Kōrei.Imperial Ministry's Bureau of Mausolea [{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 'Ryo Bo Yōran'] (1934, {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}} Digital Collection).'Mausoleum Topography Collection, Compact Edition,' Imperial Household Agency's Bureau of Mausolea, Gakuseisha, 2014, p. 400. Historical texts, including the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, reference Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto as a conqueror of Kibi Province and a participant in the {{ill|Shido Shogun|ja|四道将軍}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}}}}]] == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|（Related literature not directly used in this article）}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1988|chapter=Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} Vol. 34 Okayama Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4-582-49034-4|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Tokusho Idemiya|year=1989|chapter=Chausuyama Kofun > Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tōkyōdō Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4-490-10260-7|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Masaki Ueda|year=|chapter=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Hikoisaseri|title=Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan names|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=978-4-642-01458-8|ref={{Harvid|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinichi Yakushiji|year=2008|chapter=Walking and Thinking Along Kibi Roads Vol. 1|title=吉備の古墳 上|publisher=Kibito Publishing|isbn=|ref=}} == Related Links == {{Commonscat|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun}} * [http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] - revision.text:
{{ill|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|ja|中山茶臼山古墳}} {{Infobox ancient site |name = Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun |image = [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound (rear circular section on the right, front rectangular section on the left) |affiliation = |location = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}} {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}} {{ill|Kita-ku (Okayama)|ja|北区}} Kibitsu |latitude_degrees = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 0.51 |longitude_degrees = 133|longitude_minutes = 51|longitude_seconds = 16.87 |ISO = JP-33 |type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size = Mound length: 105 m
Height: 11 m (rear circular section) |construction_period = Early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} |burial_facility = Unknown |buried_person = (Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命}} |artifacts = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |mausoleum = Designated as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命}} by the Imperial Household Agency |noteworthy_features = Identified as half the proportional size of {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}} ({{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}) |map = Japan Okayama |label = Nakayama Chausuyama
Kofun |icon = Zenpokoenfun-South |label_position = top }} [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship site}} for Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto's tomb}}]] '''Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun''' (中山茶臼山古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kita-ku (Okayama)|ja|北区}}, {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus). The actual person buried here is unknown, but the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the mound as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命}}, a son of Emperor Kōrei, the 7th emperor of Japan. == Overview == Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus built on the summit of the independent hill range known as "Kibi no Nakayama" to the west of Okayama City. The mound is exceptionally well-preserved to this day.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} It is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}, which conducted a mound measurement survey in 2008.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus features a "paddle-shaped" keyhole design, with the front rectangular section facing south.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound is constructed in two tiers.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} Surface features include paving stones and evidence suggesting the presence of a haniwa (clay figurine) row based on haniwa fragments.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} However, further details remain unclear. The mound is estimated to have been built during the early Kofun period, though precise dating remains uncertain due to limited evidence.[http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] (Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Cultural Properties"). Its location combines coastal views of Kibitsu and inland plains, suggesting both maritime and inland significance.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus is situated on the historical boundary between Bizen and Bitchū Provinces. Other nearby tumuli in Kibi no Nakayama include early-period tumuli such as {{ill|Yatōji Yama Kofun|ja|矢藤治山古墳}} and mid-period tumuli such as {{ill|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|ja|尾上車山古墳}} (a designated national historic site). == Historical Record == * 1874 (Meiji 7): Designated as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Excavation monitoring during torii reconstruction; Yayoi pottery discovered.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 2008: Mound measurement survey conducted by the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} == Mound Dimensions == [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Rear mound of the tumulus}}{{small|Foreground: An Observation Kannon shrine at Hattoku-ji Okuin, speculated to be a former {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}}.}}]] The mound's dimensions, based on Imperial Household Agency measurements, are as follows:{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * Mound length: 105 m * Rear circular section: Elliptical (axes misaligned with the main axis of the mound). Two tiers. ** Diameter: Long axis 68 m, short axis 64 m ** Height: 11 m * Front rectangular section: Two tiers. ** Length: 63 m ** Width: Approximately 45 m ** Height: 5 m * Constriction section: ** Width: Approximately 23 m Earlier measurements estimated a mound length of approximately 120 m, but the 2008 survey revised this to the above dimensions.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound's design is considered to mirror half the proportional size of the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}} == Buried Person == The actual buried individual remains unknown. However, the Imperial Household Agency designates the mound as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto, a son of Emperor Kōrei.Imperial Ministry's Bureau of Mausolea [{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 'Ryo Bo Yōran'] (1934, {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}} Digital Collection).'Mausoleum Topography Collection, Compact Edition,' Imperial Household Agency's Bureau of Mausolea, Gakuseisha, 2014, p. 400. Historical texts, including the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, reference Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto as a conqueror of Kibi Province and a participant in the {{ill|Shido Shogun|ja|四道将軍}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}}}}]] == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|（Related literature not directly used in this article）}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1988|chapter=Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} Vol. 34 Okayama Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4-582-49034-4|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Tokusho Idemiya|year=1989|chapter=Chausuyama Kofun > Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tōkyōdō Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4-490-10260-7|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Masaki Ueda|year=|chapter=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Hikoisaseri|title=Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan names|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=978-4-642-01458-8|ref={{Harvid|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinichi Yakushiji|year=2008|chapter=Walking and Thinking Along Kibi Roads Vol. 1|title=吉備の古墳 上|publisher=Kibito Publishing|isbn=|ref=}} == Related Links == {{Commonscat|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun}} * [http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] - Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Cultural Properties" [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kita-ku, Okayama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Imperial Mausolea of Japan]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[ja:中山茶臼山古墳]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] {{Kofun navbar}} [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:%E4%B8%AD%E5%B1%B1%E8%8C%B6%E8%87%BC%E5%B1%B1%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3]] [[Category:New pages most likely untranslated or similar]] [[Category:Qq-1月4日 (旧暦)-qq]][[sn:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:new pages]] [[sn:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun}}

revision.text:
{{ill|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|ja|中山茶臼山古墳}} {{Infobox ancient site |name = Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun |image = [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound (rear circular section on the right, front rectangular section on the left) |affiliation = |location = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}} {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}} {{ill|Kita-ku (Okayama)|ja|北区}} Kibitsu |latitude_degrees = 34|latitude_minutes = 40|latitude_seconds = 0.51 |longitude_degrees = 133|longitude_minutes = 51|longitude_seconds = 16.87 |ISO = JP-33 |type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size = Mound length: 105 m
Height: 11 m (rear circular section) |construction_period = Early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} |burial_facility = Unknown |buried_person = (Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命}} |artifacts = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |mausoleum = Designated as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命}} by the Imperial Household Agency |noteworthy_features = Identified as half the proportional size of {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}} ({{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}) |map = Japan Okayama |label = Nakayama Chausuyama
Kofun |icon = Zenpokoenfun-South |label_position = top }} [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship site}} for Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto's tomb}}]] '''Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun''' (中山茶臼山古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kita-ku (Okayama)|ja|北区}}, {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. It is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus). The actual person buried here is unknown, but the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} has designated the mound as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命}}, a son of Emperor Kōrei, the 7th emperor of Japan. == Overview == Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus built on the summit of the independent hill range known as "Kibi no Nakayama" to the west of Okayama City. The mound is exceptionally well-preserved to this day.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} It is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}, which conducted a mound measurement survey in 2008.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus features a "paddle-shaped" keyhole design, with the front rectangular section facing south.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound is constructed in two tiers.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} Surface features include paving stones and evidence suggesting the presence of a haniwa (clay figurine) row based on haniwa fragments.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} However, further details remain unclear. The mound is estimated to have been built during the early Kofun period, though precise dating remains uncertain due to limited evidence.[http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] (Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Cultural Properties"). Its location combines coastal views of Kibitsu and inland plains, suggesting both maritime and inland significance.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus is situated on the historical boundary between Bizen and Bitchū Provinces. Other nearby tumuli in Kibi no Nakayama include early-period tumuli such as {{ill|Yatōji Yama Kofun|ja|矢藤治山古墳}} and mid-period tumuli such as {{ill|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|ja|尾上車山古墳}} (a designated national historic site). == Historical Record == * 1874 (Meiji 7): Designated as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Excavation monitoring during torii reconstruction; Yayoi pottery discovered.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 2008: Mound measurement survey conducted by the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} == Mound Dimensions == [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Rear mound of the tumulus}}{{small|Foreground: An Observation Kannon shrine at Hattoku-ji Okuin, speculated to be a former {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}}.}}]] The mound's dimensions, based on Imperial Household Agency measurements, are as follows:{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * Mound length: 105 m * Rear circular section: Elliptical (axes misaligned with the main axis of the mound). Two tiers. ** Diameter: Long axis 68 m, short axis 64 m ** Height: 11 m * Front rectangular section: Two tiers. ** Length: 63 m ** Width: Approximately 45 m ** Height: 5 m * Constriction section: ** Width: Approximately 23 m Earlier measurements estimated a mound length of approximately 120 m, but the 2008 survey revised this to the above dimensions.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound's design is considered to mirror half the proportional size of the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}} == Buried Person == The actual buried individual remains unknown. However, the Imperial Household Agency designates the mound as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto, a son of Emperor Kōrei.Imperial Ministry's Bureau of Mausolea [{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 'Ryo Bo Yōran'] (1934, {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}} Digital Collection).'Mausoleum Topography Collection, Compact Edition,' Imperial Household Agency's Bureau of Mausolea, Gakuseisha, 2014, p. 400. Historical texts, including the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, reference Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto as a conqueror of Kibi Province and a participant in the {{ill|Shido Shogun|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津神社}}}}]] == Notes == {{Reflist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|（Related literature not directly used in this article）}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1988|chapter=Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} Vol. 34 Okayama Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4-582-49034-4|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Tokusho Idemiya|year=1989|chapter=Chausuyama Kofun > Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tōkyōdō Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4-490-10260-7|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Masaki Ueda|year=|chapter=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Hikoisaseri|title=Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan names|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=978-4-642-01458-8|ref={{Harvid|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinichi Yakushiji|year=2008|chapter=Walking and Thinking Along Kibi Roads Vol. 1|title=吉備の古墳 上|publisher=Kibito Publishing|isbn=|ref=}} == Related Links == {{Commonscat|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun}} * [http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] - Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Cultural Properties" [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kita-ku, Okayama]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Imperial Mausolea of Japan]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[ja:中山茶臼山古墳]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] {{Kofun navbar}} [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:%E4%B8%AD%E5%B1%B1%E8%8C%B6%E8%87%BC%E5%B1%B1%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3]] [[Category:New pages most likely untranslated or similar]] [[Category:Qq-1月4日 (旧暦)-qq]][[sn:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:completion tier 5]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[sn:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Images]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]]
