Kofun |type = Zenpokoenfun-South |map_dot_label = top |architectural_styles = {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} |commemorates = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|WD=Q65318}} }} [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} for Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto's tomb}}]] '''Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun''' (中山茶臼山古墳) is a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] located in [[:en:北區|Kita-ku (Okayama)]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. It is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped tumulus). The actual person buried here is unknown, but the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] has Historic Site1 the mound as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉備津彦命}}, a son of Emperor Kōrei, the 7th emperor of Japan. == Overview == Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus built on the summit of the independent hill range known as "Kibi no Nakayama" to the west of Okayama City. The mound is exceptionally well-preserved to this day.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} It is managed by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]], which conducted a mound measurement survey in 2008.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus features a "paddle-shaped" keyhole design, with the front rectangular section facing south.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound is constructed in two tiers.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} Surface features include paving stones and evidence suggesting the presence of a haniwa (clay figurine) row based on haniwa fragments.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} However, further details remain unclear. The mound is estimated to have been built during the early Kofun period, though precise dating remains uncertain due to limited evidence.[http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] (Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Historic Site"). Its location combines coastal views of Kibitsu and inland plains, suggesting both maritime and inland significance.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus is situated on the historical boundary between Bizen and Bitchū Provinces. Other nearby tumuli in Kibi no Nakayama include early-period tumuli such as {{ill|Yatōjiyama Kofun|lt=Yatōjiyama Kofun|lt=Yatōji Yama Kofun|ja|矢藤治山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatōji Yama Kofun|qq=}} and mid-period tumuli such as [[:en:Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun]] (a Historic Site1 national historic site). == Historical Record == * 1874 (Meiji 7): Designated as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Excavation monitoring during torii reconstruction; Yayoi pottery discovered.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 2008: Mound measurement survey conducted by the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} == Mound area == [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Rear mound of the tumulus}}{{small|Foreground: An Observation Kannon shrine at Hattoku-ji Okuin, speculated to be a typeer [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]].}}]] The mound's area, based on Imperial Household Agency measurements, are as follows:{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * Mound length: 105 m * Rear circular section: Elliptical (axes misaligned with the main axis of the mound). Two tiers. ** Diameter: Long axis 68 m, short axis 64 m ** Height: 11 m * Front rectangular section: Two tiers. ** Length: 63 m ** Width: Approximately 45 m ** Height: 5 m * Constriction section: ** Width: Approximately 23 m Earlier measurements estimated a mound length of approximately 120 m, but the 2008 survey revised this to the above area.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound's design is considered to mirror half the proportional size of the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} in Nara Prefecture.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}} == Buried Person == The actual buried individual remains unknown. However, the Imperial Household Agency designates the mound as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto, a son of Emperor Kōrei.Imperial Ministry's Bureau of Mausolea [{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 'Ryo Bo Yōran'] (1934, [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]] Digital Collection).'Mausoleum Topography Collection, Compact Edition,' Imperial Household Agency's Bureau of Mausolea, Gakuseisha, 2014, p. 400. Historical texts, including the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], reference Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto as a conqueror of Kibi Province and a participant in the {{ill|Shido Shogun|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shido Shogun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|[[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]}}]] == notes == {{Reflist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == Yakushiji|year=2008|chapter=Walking and Thinking Along Kibi Roads Vol. 1|title=吉備の古墳 上|publisher=Kibito Publishing|isbn=|ref=}} == Related Links == {{Commonscat|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun}} * So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. It is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped tumulus). The mound is exceptionally well-preserved to this day.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} It is managed by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]], which conducted a mound measurement survey in 2008.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus features a "paddle-shaped" keyhole design, with the front rectangular section facing south.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound is constructed in two tiers.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} Surface features include paving stones and evidence suggesting the presence of a haniwa (clay figurine) row based on haniwa fragments.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} However, further details remain unclear. Its location combines coastal views of Kibitsu and inland plains, suggesting both maritime and inland significance.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus is situated on the historical boundary between Bizen and Bitchū Provinces. Two tiers. ** Diameter: Long axis 68 m, short axis 64 m ** Height: 11 m * Front rectangular section: Two tiers. ** Length: 63 m ** Width: Approximately 45 m ** Height: 5 m * Constriction section: ** Width: Approximately 23 m Earlier measurements estimated a mound length of approximately 120 m, but the 2008 survey revised this to the above area.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound's design is considered to mirror half the proportional size of the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} in Nara Prefecture.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}} == Buried Person == The actual buried individual remains unknown. However, the Imperial Household Agency designates the mound as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto, a son of Emperor Kōrei.Imperial Ministry's Bureau of Mausolea [{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 'Ryo Bo Yōran'] (1934, [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]] Digital Collection).'Mausoleum Topography Collection, Compact Edition,' Imperial Household Agency's Bureau of Mausolea, Gakuseisha, 2014, p. 400. Shogun|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shido Shogun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Height: 11 m (rear circular section) |built = Early [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] |burial = Unknown |buried_person = (Designated by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]) {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉備津彦命}} |burial goods = Fragments of [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] |mausoleum = Designated as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉備津彦命}} by the Imperial Household Agency |noteworthy_features = Identified as half the proportional size of {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} ([[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]]) |map = Japan Okayama |map_dot_label = Nakayama Chausuyama
Kofun |type = Zenpokoenfun-South |map_dot_label = top |architectural_styles = {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} |commemorates = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|WD=Q65318}} }} [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} for Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto's tomb}}]] '''Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun''' (中山茶臼山古墳) is a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] located in [[:en:北區|Kita-ku (Okayama)]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. It is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped tumulus). The actual person buried here is unknown, but the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] has Historic Site1 the mound as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉備津彦命}}, a son of Emperor Kōrei, the 7th emperor of Japan. == Overview == Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus built on the summit of the independent hill range known as "Kibi no Nakayama" to the west of Okayama City. The mound is exceptionally well-preserved to this day.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} It is managed by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]], which conducted a mound measurement survey in 2008.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus features a "paddle-shaped" keyhole design, with the front rectangular section facing south.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound is constructed in two tiers.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} Surface features include paving stones and evidence suggesting the presence of a haniwa (clay figurine) row based on haniwa fragments.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} However, further details remain unclear. The mound is estimated to have been built during the early Kofun period, though precise dating remains uncertain due to limited evidence.[http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] (Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Historic Site"). Its location combines coastal views of Kibitsu and inland plains, suggesting both maritime and inland significance.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus is situated on the historical boundary between Bizen and Bitchū Provinces. Other nearby tumuli in Kibi no Nakayama include early-period tumuli such as {{ill|Yatōjiyama Kofun|lt=Yatōjiyama Kofun|lt=Yatōji Yama Kofun|ja|矢藤治山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatōji Yama Kofun|qq=}} and mid-period tumuli such as [[:en:Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun]] (a Historic Site1 national historic site). == Historical Record == * 1874 (Meiji 7): Designated as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Excavation monitoring during torii reconstruction; Yayoi pottery discovered.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 2008: Mound measurement survey conducted by the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} == Mound area == [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Rear mound of the tumulus}}{{small|Foreground: An Observation Kannon shrine at Hattoku-ji Okuin, speculated to be a typeer [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]].}}]] The mound's area, based on Imperial Household Agency measurements, are as follows:{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * Mound length: 105 m * Rear circular section: Elliptical (axes misaligned with the main axis of the mound). Two tiers. ** Diameter: Long axis 68 m, short axis 64 m ** Height: 11 m * Front rectangular section: Two tiers. ** Length: 63 m ** Width: Approximately 45 m ** Height: 5 m * Constriction section: ** Width: Approximately 23 m Earlier measurements estimated a mound length of approximately 120 m, but the 2008 survey revised this to the above area.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound's design is considered to mirror half the proportional size of the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} in Nara Prefecture.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}} == Buried Person == The actual buried individual remains unknown. However, the Imperial Household Agency designates the mound as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto, a son of Emperor Kōrei.Imperial Ministry's Bureau of Mausolea [{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 'Ryo Bo Yōran'] (1934, [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]] Digital Collection).'Mausoleum Topography Collection, Compact Edition,' Imperial Household Agency's Bureau of Mausolea, Gakuseisha, 2014, p. 400. Historical texts, including the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], reference Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto as a conqueror of Kibi Province and a participant in the {{ill|Shido Shogun|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shido Shogun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|ja|中山茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name = Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun |image = File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg |affiliation = |location = {{ill|Kibitsu|WD=Q11412649}} |coordinatesrees = 34|coordinatesutes = 40|coordinates = {{Coord|34.666808|N|133.854686|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |coordinatesrees = 133|omitted = JP-33 |type = [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] |size = Mound length: 105 m
Height: 11 m (rear circular section) |built = Early [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] |burial = Unknown |buried_person = (Designated by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]) {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉備津彦命}} |burial goods = Fragments of [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] |mausoleum = Designated as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉備津彦命}} by the Imperial Household Agency |noteworthy_features = Identified as half the proportional size of {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} ([[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]]) |map = Japan Okayama |map_dot_label = Nakayama Chausuyama
Kofun |type = Zenpokoenfun-South |map_dot_label = top |architectural_styles = {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} |commemorates = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|WD=Q65318}} }} [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} for Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto's tomb}}]] '''Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun''' (中山茶臼山古墳) is a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] located in [[:en:北區|Kita-ku (Okayama)]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. It is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped tumulus). The actual person buried here is unknown, but the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] has Historic Site1 the mound as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉備津彦命}}, a son of Emperor Kōrei, the 7th emperor of Japan. == Overview == Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus built on the summit of the independent hill range known as "Kibi no Nakayama" to the west of Okayama City. The mound is exceptionally well-preserved to this day.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} It is managed by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]], which conducted a mound measurement survey in 2008.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus features a "paddle-shaped" keyhole design, with the front rectangular section facing south.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound is constructed in two tiers.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} Surface features include paving stones and evidence suggesting the presence of a haniwa (clay figurine) row based on haniwa fragments.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} However, further details remain unclear. The mound is estimated to have been built during the early Kofun period, though precise dating remains uncertain due to limited evidence.[http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] (Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Historic Site"). Its location combines coastal views of Kibitsu and inland plains, suggesting both maritime and inland significance.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus is situated on the historical boundary between Bizen and Bitchū Provinces. Other nearby tumuli in Kibi no Nakayama include early-period tumuli such as {{ill|Yatōjiyama Kofun|lt=Yatōjiyama Kofun|lt=Yatōji Yama Kofun|ja|矢藤治山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatōji Yama Kofun|qq=}} and mid-period tumuli such as [[:en:Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun]] (a Historic Site1 national historic site). == Historical Record == * 1874 (Meiji 7): Designated as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Excavation monitoring during torii reconstruction; Yayoi pottery discovered.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 2008: Mound measurement survey conducted by the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} == Mound area == [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Rear mound of the tumulus}}{{small|Foreground: An Observation Kannon shrine at Hattoku-ji Okuin, speculated to be a typeer [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]].}}]] The mound's area, based on Imperial Household Agency measurements, are as follows:{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * Mound length: 105 m * Rear circular section: Elliptical (axes misaligned with the main axis of the mound). Two tiers. ** Diameter: Long axis 68 m, short axis 64 m ** Height: 11 m * Front rectangular section: Two tiers. ** Length: 63 m ** Width: Approximately 45 m ** Height: 5 m * Constriction section: ** Width: Approximately 23 m Earlier measurements estimated a mound length of approximately 120 m, but the 2008 survey revised this to the above area.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound's design is considered to mirror half the proportional size of the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} in Nara Prefecture.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}} == Buried Person == The actual buried individual remains unknown. However, the Imperial Household Agency designates the mound as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto, a son of Emperor Kōrei.Imperial Ministry's Bureau of Mausolea [{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 'Ryo Bo Yōran'] (1934, [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]] Digital Collection).'Mausoleum Topography Collection, Compact Edition,' Imperial Household Agency's Bureau of Mausolea, Gakuseisha, 2014, p. 400. Historical texts, including the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], reference Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto as a conqueror of Kibi Province and a participant in the {{ill|Shido Shogun|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shido Shogun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|[[:en:Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]}}]] == notes == {{Reflist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == Kofun |type = Zenpokoenfun-South |map_dot_label = top |architectural_styles = {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} |commemorates = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|WD=Q65318}} }} [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} for Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto's tomb}}]] '''Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun''' (中山茶臼山古墳) is a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] located in [[:en:北區|Kita-ku (Okayama)]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. The actual person buried here is unknown, but the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] has Historic Site1 the mound as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉備津彦命}}, a son of Emperor Kōrei, the 7th emperor of Japan. == Overview == Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus built on the summit of the independent hill range known as "Kibi no Nakayama" to the west of Okayama City. The mound is estimated to have been built during the early Kofun period, though precise dating remains uncertain due to limited evidence.[http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] (Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Historic Site"). Its location combines coastal views of Kibitsu and inland plains, suggesting both maritime and inland significance.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus is situated on the historical boundary between Bizen and Bitchū Provinces. Other nearby tumuli in Kibi no Nakayama include early-period tumuli such as {{ill|Yatōjiyama Kofun|lt=Yatōjiyama Kofun|lt=Yatōji Yama Kofun|ja|矢藤治山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatōji Yama Kofun|qq=}} and mid-period tumuli such as [[:en:Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun]] (a Historic Site1 national historic site). == Historical Record == * 1874 (Meiji 7): Designated as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Excavation monitoring during torii reconstruction; Yayoi pottery discovered.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 2008: Mound measurement survey conducted by the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} == Mound area == [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Rear mound of the tumulus}}{{small|Foreground: An Observation Kannon shrine at Hattoku-ji Okuin, speculated to be a typeer [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]].}}]] The mound's area, based on Imperial Household Agency measurements, are as follows:{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * Mound length: 105 m * Rear circular section: Elliptical (axes misaligned with the main axis of the mound). Two tiers. ** Diameter: Long axis 68 m, short axis 64 m ** Height: 11 m * Front rectangular section: Two tiers. ** Length: 63 m ** Width: Approximately 45 m ** Height: 5 m * Constriction section: ** Width: Approximately 23 m Earlier measurements estimated a mound length of approximately 120 m, but the 2008 survey revised this to the above area.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound's design is considered to mirror half the proportional size of the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} in Nara Prefecture.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}} == Buried Person == The actual buried individual remains unknown. However, the Imperial Household Agency designates the mound as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto, a son of Emperor Kōrei.Imperial Ministry's Bureau of Mausolea [{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 'Ryo Bo Yōran'] (1934, [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]] Digital Collection).'Mausoleum Topography Collection, Compact Edition,' Imperial Household Agency's Bureau of Mausolea, Gakuseisha, 2014, p. 400. Historical texts, including the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], reference Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto as a conqueror of Kibi Province and a participant in the {{ill|Shido Shogun|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shido Shogun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Height: 11 m (rear circular section) |built = Early [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] |burial = Unknown |buried_person = (Designated by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]) {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉備津彦命}} |burial goods = Fragments of [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] |mausoleum = Designated as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉備津彦命}} by the Imperial Household Agency |noteworthy_features = Identified as half the proportional size of {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} ([[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]]) |map = Japan Okayama |map_dot_label = Nakayama Chausuyama
Kofun |type = Zenpokoenfun-South |map_dot_label = top |architectural_styles = {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} |commemorates = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|WD=Q65318}} }} [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} for Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto's tomb}}]] '''Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun''' (中山茶臼山古墳) is a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] located in [[:en:北區|Kita-ku (Okayama)]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. It is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped tumulus). The actual person buried here is unknown, but the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] has Historic Site1 the mound as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉備津彦命}}, a son of Emperor Kōrei, the 7th emperor of Japan. == Overview == Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus built on the summit of the independent hill range known as "Kibi no Nakayama" to the west of Okayama City. The mound is exceptionally well-preserved to this day.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} It is managed by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]], which conducted a mound measurement survey in 2008.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus features a "paddle-shaped" keyhole design, with the front rectangular section facing south.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound is constructed in two tiers.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} Surface features include paving stones and evidence suggesting the presence of a haniwa (clay figurine) row based on haniwa fragments.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} However, further details remain unclear. The mound is estimated to have been built during the early Kofun period, though precise dating remains uncertain due to limited evidence.[http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] (Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Historic Site"). Its location combines coastal views of Kibitsu and inland plains, suggesting both maritime and inland significance.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus is situated on the historical boundary between Bizen and Bitchū Provinces. Other nearby tumuli in Kibi no Nakayama include early-period tumuli such as {{ill|Yatōjiyama Kofun|lt=Yatōjiyama Kofun|lt=Yatōji Yama Kofun|ja|矢藤治山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatōji Yama Kofun|qq=}} and mid-period tumuli such as [[:en:Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun]] (a Historic Site1 national historic site). == Historical Record == * 1874 (Meiji 7): Designated as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Excavation monitoring during torii reconstruction; Yayoi pottery discovered.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 2008: Mound measurement survey conducted by the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} == Mound area == [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Rear mound of the tumulus}}{{small|Foreground: An Observation Kannon shrine at Hattoku-ji Okuin, speculated to be a typeer [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]].}}]] The mound's area, based on Imperial Household Agency measurements, are as follows:{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * Mound length: 105 m * Rear circular section: Elliptical (axes misaligned with the main axis of the mound). Two tiers. ** Diameter: Long axis 68 m, short axis 64 m ** Height: 11 m * Front rectangular section: Two tiers. ** Length: 63 m ** Width: Approximately 45 m ** Height: 5 m * Constriction section: ** Width: Approximately 23 m Earlier measurements estimated a mound length of approximately 120 m, but the 2008 survey revised this to the above area.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound's design is considered to mirror half the proportional size of the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} in Nara Prefecture.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}} == Buried Person == The actual buried individual remains unknown. However, the Imperial Household Agency designates the mound as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto, a son of Emperor Kōrei.Imperial Ministry's Bureau of Mausolea [{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 'Ryo Bo Yōran'] (1934, [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]] Digital Collection).'Mausoleum Topography Collection, Compact Edition,' Imperial Household Agency's Bureau of Mausolea, Gakuseisha, 2014, p. 400. Historical texts, including the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], reference Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto as a conqueror of Kibi Province and a participant in the {{ill|Shido Shogun|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shido Shogun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Kofun |type = Zenpokoenfun-South |map_dot_label = top |architectural_styles = {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} |commemorates = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|WD=Q65318}} }} [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} for Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto's tomb}}]] '''Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun''' (中山茶臼山古墳) is a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] located in [[:en:北區|Kita-ku (Okayama)]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. It is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped tumulus). The actual person buried here is unknown, but the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] has Historic Site1 the mound as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉備津彦命}}, a son of Emperor Kōrei, the 7th emperor of Japan. == Overview == Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus built on the summit of the independent hill range known as "Kibi no Nakayama" to the west of Okayama City. The mound is exceptionally well-preserved to this day.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} It is managed by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]], which conducted a mound measurement survey in 2008.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus features a "paddle-shaped" keyhole design, with the front rectangular section facing south.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound is constructed in two tiers.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} Surface features include paving stones and evidence suggesting the presence of a haniwa (clay figurine) row based on haniwa fragments.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} However, further details remain unclear. The mound is estimated to have been built during the early Kofun period, though precise dating remains uncertain due to limited evidence.[http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] (Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Historic Site"). Its location combines coastal views of Kibitsu and inland plains, suggesting both maritime and inland significance.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus is situated on the historical boundary between Bizen and Bitchū Provinces. Other nearby tumuli in Kibi no Nakayama include early-period tumuli such as {{ill|Yatōjiyama Kofun|lt=Yatōjiyama Kofun|lt=Yatōji Yama Kofun|ja|矢藤治山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatōji Yama Kofun|qq=}} and mid-period tumuli such as [[:en:Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun]] (a Historic Site1 national historic site). == Historical Record == * 1874 (Meiji 7): Designated as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Excavation monitoring during torii reconstruction; Yayoi pottery discovered.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 2008: Mound measurement survey conducted by the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} == Mound area == [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Rear mound of the tumulus}}{{small|Foreground: An Observation Kannon shrine at Hattoku-ji Okuin, speculated to be a typeer [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]].}}]] The mound's area, based on Imperial Household Agency measurements, are as follows:{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * Mound length: 105 m * Rear circular section: Elliptical (axes misaligned with the main axis of the mound). Two tiers. ** Diameter: Long axis 68 m, short axis 64 m ** Height: 11 m * Front rectangular section: Two tiers. ** Length: 63 m ** Width: Approximately 45 m ** Height: 5 m * Constriction section: ** Width: Approximately 23 m Earlier measurements estimated a mound length of approximately 120 m, but the 2008 survey revised this to the above area.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound's design is considered to mirror half the proportional size of the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} in Nara Prefecture.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}} == Buried Person == The actual buried individual remains unknown. However, the Imperial Household Agency designates the mound as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto, a son of Emperor Kōrei.Imperial Ministry's Bureau of Mausolea [{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 'Ryo Bo Yōran'] (1934, [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]] Digital Collection).'Mausoleum Topography Collection, Compact Edition,' Imperial Household Agency's Bureau of Mausolea, Gakuseisha, 2014, p. 400. Historical texts, including the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], reference Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto as a conqueror of Kibi Province and a participant in the {{ill|Shido Shogun|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shido Shogun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Kofun}} {{wikidata link|Q11364273}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Kita-ku, Okayama]] [[Category:Japanese imperial tombs]] [[Category:Kofun of Okayama prefecture]] [[co:commons:Category:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[ja:中山茶臼山古墳]] [[co:commons:Category:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[ja:中山茶臼山古墳]] [[sn:commons:Category:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[ja:中山茶臼山古墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11364273]] Wikidata name: Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=See as this is a member[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 22:44, 22 April 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|ja|中山茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name = Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun |image = File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg |affiliation = |location = {{ill|Kibitsu|WD=Q11412649}} |coordinatesrees = 34|coordinatesutes = 40|coordinates = {{Coord|34.666808|N|133.854686|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |coordinatesrees = 133|omitted = JP-33 |type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} |size = Mound length: 105 m
Height: 11 m (rear circular section) |built = Early {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}} |burial = Unknown |buried_person = (Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}}) {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉備津彦命}} |burial goods = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}} |mausoleum = Designated as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉備津彦命}} by the Imperial Household Agency |noteworthy_features = Identified as half the proportional size of {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} ({{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}, {{ill|Tenri, Nara|en|Tenri, Nara|WD=Q502196|lt=Tenri}}) |map = Japan Okayama |map_dot_label = Nakayama Chausuyama
Kofun |type = Zenpokoenfun-South |map_dot_label = top |architectural_styles = {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} |commemorates = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|WD=Q65318}} }} [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} for Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto's tomb}}]] '''Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun''' (中山茶臼山古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}} located in {{ill|北區|en|北區|WD=Q1180096|lt=Kita-ku (Okayama)}}, {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus). The actual person buried here is unknown, but the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} has Historic Site1 the mound as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉備津彦命}}, a son of Emperor Kōrei, the 7th emperor of Japan. == Overview == Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus built on the summit of the independent hill range known as "Kibi no Nakayama" to the west of Okayama City. The mound is exceptionally well-preserved to this day.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} It is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}}, which conducted a mound measurement survey in 2008.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus features a "paddle-shaped" keyhole design, with the front rectangular section facing south.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound is constructed in two tiers.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} Surface features include paving stones and evidence suggesting the presence of a haniwa (clay figurine) row based on haniwa fragments.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} However, further details remain unclear. The mound is estimated to have been built during the early Kofun period, though precise dating remains uncertain due to limited evidence.[http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] (Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Historic Site"). Its location combines coastal views of Kibitsu and inland plains, suggesting both maritime and inland significance.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus is situated on the historical boundary between Bizen and Bitchū Provinces. Other nearby tumuli in Kibi no Nakayama include early-period tumuli such as {{ill|Yatōjiyama Kofun|lt=Yatōjiyama Kofun|lt=Yatōji Yama Kofun|ja|矢藤治山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatōji Yama Kofun|qq=}} and mid-period tumuli such as {{ill|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|en|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|WD=Q28685132|lt=Onoe Kurumayama Kofun}} (a Historic Site1 national historic site). == Historical Record == * 1874 (Meiji 7): Designated as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Excavation monitoring during torii reconstruction; Yayoi pottery discovered.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 2008: Mound measurement survey conducted by the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} == Mound area == [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Rear mound of the tumulus}}{{small|Foreground: An Observation Kannon shrine at Hattoku-ji Okuin, speculated to be a typeer {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=Iwakura (Shinto)}}.}}]] The mound's area, based on Imperial Household Agency measurements, are as follows:{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * Mound length: 105 m * Rear circular section: Elliptical (axes misaligned with the main axis of the mound). Two tiers. ** Diameter: Long axis 68 m, short axis 64 m ** Height: 11 m * Front rectangular section: Two tiers. ** Length: 63 m ** Width: Approximately 45 m ** Height: 5 m * Constriction section: ** Width: Approximately 23 m Earlier measurements estimated a mound length of approximately 120 m, but the 2008 survey revised this to the above area.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound's design is considered to mirror half the proportional size of the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} in Nara Prefecture.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}} == Buried Person == The actual buried individual remains unknown. However, the Imperial Household Agency designates the mound as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto, a son of Emperor Kōrei.Imperial Ministry's Bureau of Mausolea [{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 'Ryo Bo Yōran'] (1934, {{ill|National Diet Library|en|National Diet Library|WD=Q477675|lt=National Diet Library}} Digital Collection).'Mausoleum Topography Collection, Compact Edition,' Imperial Household Agency's Bureau of Mausolea, Gakuseisha, 2014, p. 400. Historical texts, including the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, reference Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto as a conqueror of Kibi Province and a participant in the {{ill|Shido Shogun|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shido Shogun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|en|Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|WD=Q712824|lt=Kibitsu Shrine}}}}]] == notes == {{Reflist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|（Related literature not directly used in this article）}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1988|chapter=Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1=Nihon rekishi chimei taikei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213509}} Vol. 34 Okayama Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|WD=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4-582-49034-4|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Tokusho Idemiya|year=1989|chapter=Chausuyama Kofun > Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kofun Daijiten|13=User:Immanuelle/Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|qq=}}|publisher={{ill|Tōkyōdō Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkyōdō Shuppan|1=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524645}}|isbn=4-490-10260-7|ref={{Harvid|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Masaki Ueda|year=|chapter=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=Hikoisaseri|title=Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan names|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}|isbn=978-4-642-01458-8|ref={{Harvid|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shinichi Yakushiji|year=2008|chapter=Walking and Thinking Along Kibi Roads Vol. 1|title=吉備の古墳 上|publisher=Kibito Publishing|isbn=|ref=}} == Related Links == {{Commonscat|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun}} * [http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] - Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Historic Site" {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated Kofun}} {{wikidata link|Q11364273}} [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Kita-ku, Okayama]] [[Category:Japanese imperial tombs]] [[Category:Kofun of Okayama prefecture]] Kofun |type = Zenpokoenfun-South |map_dot_label = top |architectural_styles = {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} |commemorates = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|WD=Q65318}} }} [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} for Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto's tomb}}]] '''Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun''' (中山茶臼山古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}} located in {{ill|北區|en|北區|WD=Q1180096|lt=Kita-ku (Okayama)}}, {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus). The actual person buried here is unknown, but the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} has Historic Site1 the mound as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉備津彦命}}, a son of Emperor Kōrei, the 7th emperor of Japan. == Overview == Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus built on the summit of the independent hill range known as "Kibi no Nakayama" to the west of Okayama City. The mound is exceptionally well-preserved to this day.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} It is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}}, which conducted a mound measurement survey in 2008.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus features a "paddle-shaped" keyhole design, with the front rectangular section facing south.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound is constructed in two tiers.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} Surface features include paving stones and evidence suggesting the presence of a haniwa (clay figurine) row based on haniwa fragments.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} However, further details remain unclear. The mound is estimated to have been built during the early Kofun period, though precise dating remains uncertain due to limited evidence.[http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] (Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Historic Site"). Its location combines coastal views of Kibitsu and inland plains, suggesting both maritime and inland significance.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus is situated on the historical boundary between Bizen and Bitchū Provinces. Other nearby tumuli in Kibi no Nakayama include early-period tumuli such as {{ill|Yatōjiyama Kofun|lt=Yatōjiyama Kofun|lt=Yatōji Yama Kofun|ja|矢藤治山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatōji Yama Kofun|qq=}} and mid-period tumuli such as {{ill|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|en|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|WD=Q28685132|lt=Onoe Kurumayama Kofun}} (a Historic Site1 national historic site). == Historical Record == * 1874 (Meiji 7): Designated as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Excavation monitoring during torii reconstruction; Yayoi pottery discovered.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 2008: Mound measurement survey conducted by the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} == Mound area == [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Rear mound of the tumulus}}{{small|Foreground: An Observation Kannon shrine at Hattoku-ji Okuin, speculated to be a typeer {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=Iwakura (Shinto)}}.}}]] The mound's area, based on Imperial Household Agency measurements, are as follows:{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * Mound length: 105 m * Rear circular section: Elliptical (axes misaligned with the main axis of the mound). Two tiers. ** Diameter: Long axis 68 m, short axis 64 m ** Height: 11 m * Front rectangular section: Two tiers. ** Length: 63 m ** Width: Approximately 45 m ** Height: 5 m * Constriction section: ** Width: Approximately 23 m Earlier measurements estimated a mound length of approximately 120 m, but the 2008 survey revised this to the above area.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound's design is considered to mirror half the proportional size of the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} in Nara Prefecture.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}} == Buried Person == The actual buried individual remains unknown. However, the Imperial Household Agency designates the mound as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto, a son of Emperor Kōrei.Imperial Ministry's Bureau of Mausolea [{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 'Ryo Bo Yōran'] (1934, {{ill|National Diet Library|en|National Diet Library|WD=Q477675|lt=National Diet Library}} Digital Collection).'Mausoleum Topography Collection, Compact Edition,' Imperial Household Agency's Bureau of Mausolea, Gakuseisha, 2014, p. 400. Historical texts, including the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, reference Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto as a conqueror of Kibi Province and a participant in the {{ill|Shido Shogun|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shido Shogun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|en|Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|WD=Q712824|lt=Kibitsu Shrine}}}}]] == notes == {{Reflist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|（Related literature not directly used in this article）}} * {{Cite [[co:commons:Category:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[ja:中山茶臼山古墳]] [[co:commons:Category:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[ja:中山茶臼山古墳]] [[sn:commons:Category:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun]][[ja:中山茶臼山古墳]] {{ill|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|ja|中山茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name = Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun |image = File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg |affiliation = |location = {{ill|Kibitsu|WD=Q11412649}} |coordinatesrees = 34|coordinatesutes = 40|coordinates = {{Coord|34.666808|N|133.854686|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |coordinatesrees = 133|omitted = JP-33 |type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} |size = Mound length: 105 m
Height: 11 m (rear circular section) |built = Early {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}} |burial = Unknown |buried_person = (Designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}}) {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉備津彦命}} |burial goods = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}} |mausoleum = Designated as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉備津彦命}} by the Imperial Household Agency |noteworthy_features = Identified as half the proportional size of {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} ({{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}, {{ill|Tenri, Nara|en|Tenri, Nara|WD=Q502196|lt=Tenri}}) |map = Japan Okayama |map_dot_label = Nakayama Chausuyama
Kofun |type = Zenpokoenfun-South |map_dot_label = top |architectural_styles = {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} |commemorates = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|WD=Q65318}} }} [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} for Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto's tomb}}]] '''Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun''' (中山茶臼山古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}} located in {{ill|北區|en|北區|WD=Q1180096|lt=Kita-ku (Okayama)}}, {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus). The actual person buried here is unknown, but the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} has Historic Site1 the mound as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉備津彦命}}, a son of Emperor Kōrei, the 7th emperor of Japan. == Overview == Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus built on the summit of the independent hill range known as "Kibi no Nakayama" to the west of Okayama City. The mound is exceptionally well-preserved to this day.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} It is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}}, which conducted a mound measurement survey in 2008.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus features a "paddle-shaped" keyhole design, with the front rectangular section facing south.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound is constructed in two tiers.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} Surface features include paving stones and evidence suggesting the presence of a haniwa (clay figurine) row based on haniwa fragments.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} However, further details remain unclear. The mound is estimated to have been built during the early Kofun period, though precise dating remains uncertain due to limited evidence.[http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] (Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Historic Site"). Its location combines coastal views of Kibitsu and inland plains, suggesting both maritime and inland significance.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus is situated on the historical boundary between Bizen and Bitchū Provinces. Other nearby tumuli in Kibi no Nakayama include early-period tumuli such as {{ill|Yatōjiyama Kofun|lt=Yatōjiyama Kofun|lt=Yatōji Yama Kofun|ja|矢藤治山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatōji Yama Kofun|qq=}} and mid-period tumuli such as {{ill|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|en|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|WD=Q28685132|lt=Onoe Kurumayama Kofun}} (a Historic Site1 national historic site). == Historical Record == * 1874 (Meiji 7): Designated as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Excavation monitoring during torii reconstruction; Yayoi pottery discovered.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 2008: Mound measurement survey conducted by the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} == Mound area == [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Rear mound of the tumulus}}{{small|Foreground: An Observation Kannon shrine at Hattoku-ji Okuin, speculated to be a typeer {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=Iwakura (Shinto)}}.}}]] The mound's area, based on Imperial Household Agency measurements, are as follows:{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * Mound length: 105 m * Rear circular section: Elliptical (axes misaligned with the main axis of the mound). Two tiers. ** Diameter: Long axis 68 m, short axis 64 m ** Height: 11 m * Front rectangular section: Two tiers. ** Length: 63 m ** Width: Approximately 45 m ** Height: 5 m * Constriction section: ** Width: Approximately 23 m Earlier measurements estimated a mound length of approximately 120 m, but the 2008 survey revised this to the above area.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound's design is considered to mirror half the proportional size of the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} in Nara Prefecture.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}} == Buried Person == The actual buried individual remains unknown. However, the Imperial Household Agency designates the mound as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto, a son of Emperor Kōrei.Imperial Ministry's Bureau of Mausolea [{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 'Ryo Bo Yōran'] (1934, {{ill|National Diet Library|en|National Diet Library|WD=Q477675|lt=National Diet Library}} Digital Collection).'Mausoleum Topography Collection, Compact Edition,' Imperial Household Agency's Bureau of Mausolea, Gakuseisha, 2014, p. 400. Historical texts, including the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, reference Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto as a conqueror of Kibi Province and a participant in the {{ill|Shido Shogun|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shido Shogun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|en|Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|WD=Q712824|lt=Kibitsu Shrine}}}}]] == notes == {{Reflist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|（Related literature not directly used in this article）}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1988|chapter=Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1=Nihon rekishi chimei Kofun |type = Zenpokoenfun-South |map_dot_label = top |architectural_styles = {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} |commemorates = {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|WD=Q65318}} }} [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}} for Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto's tomb}}]] '''Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun''' (中山茶臼山古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}} located in {{ill|北區|en|北區|WD=Q1180096|lt=Kita-ku (Okayama)}}, {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped tumulus). The actual person buried here is unknown, but the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} has Historic Site1 the mound as the tomb of {{ill|Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|lt=Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto|ja|大吉備津彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsuhiko-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉備津彦命}}, a son of Emperor Kōrei, the 7th emperor of Japan. == Overview == Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun is a large keyhole-shaped tumulus built on the summit of the independent hill range known as "Kibi no Nakayama" to the west of Okayama City. The mound is exceptionally well-preserved to this day.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} It is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}}, which conducted a mound measurement survey in 2008.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus features a "paddle-shaped" keyhole design, with the front rectangular section facing south.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound is constructed in two tiers.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} Surface features include paving stones and evidence suggesting the presence of a haniwa (clay figurine) row based on haniwa fragments.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} However, further details remain unclear. The mound is estimated to have been built during the early Kofun period, though precise dating remains uncertain due to limited evidence.[http://www.city.okayama.jp/museum/kofun1/09.html Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun] (Okayama City Museum "Explore Okayama's Historic Site"). Its location combines coastal views of Kibitsu and inland plains, suggesting both maritime and inland significance.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}}{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The tumulus is situated on the historical boundary between Bizen and Bitchū Provinces. Other nearby tumuli in Kibi no Nakayama include early-period tumuli such as {{ill|Yatōjiyama Kofun|lt=Yatōjiyama Kofun|lt=Yatōji Yama Kofun|ja|矢藤治山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatōji Yama Kofun|qq=}} and mid-period tumuli such as {{ill|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|en|Onoe Kurumayama Kofun|WD=Q28685132|lt=Onoe Kurumayama Kofun}} (a Historic Site1 national historic site). == Historical Record == * 1874 (Meiji 7): Designated as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 1980 (Shōwa 55): Excavation monitoring during torii reconstruction; Yayoi pottery discovered.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * 2008: Mound measurement survey conducted by the Imperial Household Agency.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} == Mound area == [[File:Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Rear mound of the tumulus}}{{small|Foreground: An Observation Kannon shrine at Hattoku-ji Okuin, speculated to be a typeer {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=Iwakura (Shinto)}}.}}]] The mound's area, based on Imperial Household Agency measurements, are as follows:{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} * Mound length: 105 m * Rear circular section: Elliptical (axes misaligned with the main axis of the mound). Two tiers. ** Diameter: Long axis 68 m, short axis 64 m ** Height: 11 m * Front rectangular section: Two tiers. ** Length: 63 m ** Width: Approximately 45 m ** Height: 5 m * Constriction section: ** Width: Approximately 23 m Earlier measurements estimated a mound length of approximately 120 m, but the 2008 survey revised this to the above area.{{Sfn|Shoryobu Journal, Mausoleum Section Vol. 61|2010|pp=21-29}} The mound's design is considered to mirror half the proportional size of the {{ill|Andon'yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳|1=Andon-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon'yama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Andon-yama Kofun|qq=}} in Nara Prefecture.{{Sfn|Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)|1988}} == Buried Person == The actual buried individual remains unknown. However, the Imperial Household Agency designates the mound as the tomb of Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto, a son of Emperor Kōrei.Imperial Ministry's Bureau of Mausolea [{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 'Ryo Bo Yōran'] (1934, {{ill|National Diet Library|en|National Diet Library|WD=Q477675|lt=National Diet Library}} Digital Collection).'Mausoleum Topography Collection, Compact Edition,' Imperial Household Agency's Bureau of Mausolea, Gakuseisha, 2014, p. 400. Historical texts, including the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, reference Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto as a conqueror of Kibi Province and a participant in the {{ill|Shido Shogun|ja|四道将軍|1=Shidō Shōgun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shido Shogun|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidō Shōgun|qq=}} campaign.{{Sfn|Ōkibitsuhiko no Mikoto (Kokushi)|}}{{Sfn|Hikoisaseri (Ancient Clans)|2010}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|en|Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|WD=Q712824|lt=Kibitsu Shrine}}}}]] == notes == {{Reflist}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|（Related literature not directly used in this article）}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1988|chapter=Nakayama Chausuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1=Nihon rekishi chimei Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kofun Daijiten|13=User:Immanuelle/Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese
